name: DraftKings
summary: Daily fantasy sports pioneer that pivoted into legal online sports betting after PASPA fell in 2018, becoming a dominant US sportsbook via SPAC merger.
lesson: Regulatory change can turn a niche DFS operator into a category-defining public company if it moves fast.

name: Sportradar
summary: Swiss sports data company supplying official league data and integrity monitoring to betting operators and media, now a public company partnered with the NBA and NHL.
lesson: Becoming the trusted data layer between leagues and betting operators creates a defensible two-sided platform.

name: Hudl
summary: Video analysis and scouting software used by high school through pro sports teams, grew into a category-dominant platform without ever going public.
lesson: Owning the workflow of an entire sport's coaching ecosystem can sustain growth for two decades without an IPO.

name: Strava
summary: Social fitness tracking network for runners and cyclists that built a durable subscription business around activity-sharing and leaderboards.
lesson: A social graph built around a niche activity can outlast many better-funded fitness hardware competitors.

name: WHOOP
summary: Subscription-based fitness and recovery wearable used by professional athletes and consumers, avoiding hardware-margin dependence via a membership model.
lesson: A subscription model for wearables avoids the boom-bust hardware-margin trap that killed earlier fitness wearables.

name: Palantir
summary: Data analytics platform originally built for intelligence and defense agencies that expanded into broader government and commercial contracts.
lesson: Deep early alignment with government's most demanding data problems built trust that later expanded into commercial markets.

name: Anduril
summary: Defense technology company building autonomous systems and surveillance towers for the US government, becoming one of the most valuable defense startups ever.
lesson: Building hardware-plus-software defense products with venture speed can win contracts from slower legacy primes.

name: CLEAR Secure
summary: Biometric identity verification company best known for expedited airport security lanes, expanded into stadium and venue entry and health verification.
lesson: A subscription identity-verification network gets more valuable as it expands into new physical venues.

name: Flock Safety
summary: License-plate-recognition and public-safety camera network for police departments and neighborhoods, growing into one of Atlanta's most valuable tech unicorns.
lesson: Selling public-safety hardware-as-a-service to thousands of small police departments can scale into a multibillion-dollar network.

name: Tyler Technologies
summary: The largest US govtech software vendor, built its dominance through decades of acquisitions of smaller government software providers.
lesson: Systematic roll-up of niche government software vendors can build an enduring category leader.

name: Buzzer
summary: Fan app letting users stream short 99-cent clips of live sporting events, backed by Michael Jordan, shut down after a licensing pivot failed to gain traction.
lesson: Micro-transaction clip streaming couldn't out-compete free social highlights already available from leagues and broadcasters.

name: Diamond Sports Group
summary: Owner of the Bally Sports regional sports networks carrying dozens of MLB, NBA, and NHL teams, filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy after cord-cutting gutted subscriber revenue.
lesson: Leveraging up to buy legacy cable sports-rights assets was catastrophic timing just as cord-cutting accelerated.

name: Barstool Sportsbook
summary: Penn Entertainment's sports betting brand built on the Barstool Sports media property, abandoned and sold back to founder Dave Portnoy for $1 after struggling to get licensed in key states.
lesson: A controversial media personality's brand can be a liability when regulators must approve gaming licenses state by state.

name: Peloton
summary: Connected fitness hardware maker whose pandemic-era boom collapsed post-2021, wiping out over 90% of its stock value and forcing out its founder amid mass layoffs.
lesson: Scaling manufacturing and headcount for pandemic-level demand becomes a crippling liability the moment demand normalizes.

name: ShotSpotter (SoundThinking)
summary: Gunshot-detection technology used by police departments has faced a wave of city contract cancellations over accuracy and bias concerns.
lesson: Public-safety AI tools face an existential risk when independent audits and community backlash undermine trust faster than sales growth.

name: PredPol / Geolitica
summary: Predictive policing software rebranded as Geolitica ceased operations at the end of 2023 after studies found it barely more accurate than random guessing and reinforced racial bias.
lesson: Predictive policing algorithms that reinforce existing patrol bias without proven crime-reduction results eventually lose government customers.

name: Voatz
summary: Blockchain-based mobile voting app used in West Virginia elections, was abandoned by the state after MIT researchers found serious security vulnerabilities.
lesson: Mobile voting technology carries such high stakes that a single credible security audit can end government adoption overnight.

name: Sidewalk Labs
summary: Alphabet's smart-city venture cancelled its flagship Quayside waterfront redevelopment project in Toronto in 2020 amid privacy backlash and pandemic-driven economic uncertainty.
lesson: Smart-city projects that centralize resident data collection invite intense privacy and governance scrutiny that can kill a project outright.

name: Clearview AI
summary: Facial recognition company used by law enforcement has been banned or fined in Canada, the UK, France, Italy, and several US states over its unconsented scraping of billions of photos.
lesson: Building a surveillance database from scraped personal data without consent invites regulatory bans across multiple jurisdictions.

name: Geofeedia
summary: Social media surveillance tool sold to police departments collapsed after an ACLU investigation revealed it was used to monitor Black Lives Matter activists, prompting platforms to cut off its data access.
lesson: Building a business entirely dependent on social platforms' data APIs is fragile if the use case violates those platforms' policies.
